parsed_findings.chain_steps: Untrusted `dir` at line 4 is embedded into `q` at line 7 and executed by `db.fetch_all` at line 8.
parsed_findings.impact: Database contents may be exposed or manipulated depending on DB features, parser behavior, and driver query capabilities.
parsed_findings.proof: A crafted `dir` value can change query structure (e.g., extra tokens/expressions) beyond intended `asc`/`desc`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call the handler with `sort=name` and a malicious `dir` payload and observe differing SQL behavior/errors versus `dir=asc`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `dir` from `request.args` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without allowlisting, unlike `sort`.

parsed_findings.impact: Enables SSRF against internal networks, link-local/cloud metadata endpoints, and other backends reachable from the app, often leading to credential or service-data exposure.
parsed_findings.proof: A request like `GET /fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` makes the server issue that HTTP request and return the response body to the client.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `GET /fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (or another internal URL reachable from the server) and observe the proxied response in the reply body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `url` query parameter is forwarded to `axios.get` while the intended host allowlist guard on line 3 is syntactically broken, so no effective restriction is enforced.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can change the victim’s invoice note without their consent when session auth relies on ambient cookies.
parsed_findings.proof: The handler reads JSON and mutates persisted state with no shown Origin/Referer or anti-CSRF gate before db.save.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: While the victim is logged in, load an attacker page that triggers a cross-site PATCH to this URL with a JSON body setting note.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: This state-changing PATCH handler has no visible CSRF token, double-submit cookie, or SameSite/session binding, so browser cookie sessions can be forged from another site.

parsed_findings.impact: Editors may receive or leak fields (amounts, PII, internal metadata) they should not see.
parsed_findings.proof: The response uses a whole-object serializer after a partial update without field scoping in the snippet.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a PATCH updating only note and inspect the JSON response for additional sensitive keys.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Returning inv.to_json() may expose the full invoice record beyond the updated note field.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: None; traversal alone yields arbitrary read within OS permissions.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file disclosure for paths readable by the Node process.
parsed_findings.proof: Input like ....//....// leaves residual .. segments after one replace, which path.join normalizes into parent-directory escapes from the base path.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send GET /download?file=....//....//etc/passwd (or multiple ../ before/after padding) and observe reads outside /srv/data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: A single string replace of '../' removes only one occurrence and misses equivalent sequences, so path.join can still resolve outside /srv/data.

parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary code execution on the server with the privileges of the web application process.
parsed_findings.proof: `pickle` can execute arbitrary Python during unpickling when given a crafted payload, so untrusted input must never be unpickled.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Craft a malicious pickle, base64-encode it, set cookie `state` to that value, and request `GET /resume` to trigger unpickling.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `state` cookie is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, which deserializes attacker-controlled data.

parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation or integrity loss by overwriting security-relevant user attributes.
parsed_findings.proof: A PATCH body can include privileged properties that copy onto `user` and are written by `db.users.save(user)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/users/:id` with a JSON body containing sensitive user properties and verify they persist on the target record.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Merging unvalidated `req.body` into the loaded user with `Object.assign` then saving persists attacker-chosen fields such as roles or flags.

parsed_findings.impact: Intended access restrictions fail, enabling cross-user profile updates when the route is reachable.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 concatenates `req.user.id` and `req.user.isSupport` without a relational operator or `req.params.id` comparison, so it cannot enforce self-or-support access to the `:id` resource.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Attempt `PATCH /api/users/<anotherUserId>` and observe whether the handler proceeds to `findById` and `save` without a correct ownership/support predicate.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The purported authorization guard is not a coherent check comparing the authenticated subject to `req.params.id` before loading and updating that user.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject HTML in `body` → `rendered` retains it → DB stores `html` → `list_comments` returns it to clients that render the field.
parsed_findings.impact: Stored XSS can hijack victim sessions or perform actions as the viewing user.
parsed_findings.proof: A POST body like `` survives `markdown.markdown` and is persisted as `html` for other users to load.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with `{"body":""}` then fetch `/comments` and open the returned `html` in a browser context.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Markdown-to-HTML conversion stores attacker-controlled markup that python-markdown typically passes through, enabling stored HTML/JS if later rendered unsafely.

parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary balance manipulation and theft from the chosen recipient.
parsed_findings.proof: For positive src and amt=-100, src<amt is false, src-amt increases the sender balance, and the recipient balance is reduced by 100.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Accepting a negative amount skips the insufficient-funds guard and reverses the debit/credit, inflating the sender and stealing from the recipient.

parsed_findings.impact: Double-spend or insolvency against the wallet ledger.
parsed_findings.proof: Two parallel requests can both read src before either write, each pass src<amt, and both debits apply, overdrawing the account.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue two simultaneous POST /transfer requests whose amounts sum above the current balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Non-atomic read-check-write allows concurrent transfers to observe the same balance and collectively exceed available funds.

parsed_findings.impact: Authentication bypass allowing attackers to forge identities or privileges via tampered JWTs.
parsed_findings.proof: decode parses tokens without signature verification, so forged Bearer tokens become trusted after verify throws.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set NODE_ENV to production, send a Bearer JWT with an invalid signature and arbitrary claims, observe decoded claims returned.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, failed jwt.verify falls back to jwt.decode, which returns an unverified payload.

parsed_findings.impact: Sensitive user emails and identifiers can be enumerated and exfiltrated at scale.
parsed_findings.proof: A client can call GET /u?id=N and receive the corresponding users row from the database without any identity check in this code.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send GET /u?id=1 (and increment N) and observe id and email returned for arbitrary users.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler loads any user by supplied id with no authentication or authorization, enabling unauthenticated IDOR reads of id and email.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Recover/forged `X-Sig` enables calling `process_event` with attacker-controlled JSON.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote attacker may iteratively recover a valid signature and bypass authentication.
parsed_findings.proof: Non-constant-time string comparison of `got` and `want` differs from `hmac.compare_digest` semantics.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many crafted `X-Sig` values and correlate response latency with matching prefix length.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: HMAC tags are compared with `==`, which is not constant-time and can leak via timing.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Resource exhaustion can degrade availability independent of signature forgery.
parsed_findings.impact: Unauthenticated clients can exhaust server memory or CPU, causing denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: Large POST bodies are accepted and buffered even when `X-Sig` is invalid or missing.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST a very large body with a wrong `X-Sig` and observe memory/CPU saturation on the service.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The full body is read with `get_data()` before signature verification completes.
